  • Seeks to use both quantitative insights and fundamental analysis to uncover investment opportunities.
  • Evaluates factors like management strength, industry standing, and risk profiles to confirm each stock's potential.

Quarterly review

  • The Large Cap Core Fund underperformed the S&P 500 Index benchmark during the fourth quarter.
  • For the three-month period, industrials, information technology (IT), and energy detracted from relative performance.
  • Financials, real estate, and utilities contributed to relative performance.

Market review

The S&P 500 Index advanced 17.88% during 2025, notching a third consecutive year above 15% performance for the first time since 2021. U.S. equities extended their gains for a third consecutive quarter, with the S&P 500 Index rising 2.66% and setting multiple record closing highs during the fourth quarter. U.S. small- and mid-cap stocks were also positive during the quarter, with the Russell 2000 Index gaining 2.19% and the Russell 2500 Index returning 2.22%. The broadening of market returns was supported by solid corporate earnings, continued enthusiasm around artificial intelligence (AI), a more dovish U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed), easing trade tensions, and resilient consumer sentiment. International equity markets fared even better, with the MSCI World Index ex USA (NET) rising 5.05% and the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (NET) returning 4.73%. U.S. large caps outperformed small caps, value outperformed growth, and West Texas Intermediate crude oil prices fell for a fourth straight quarter, ending the quarter down 9.1%.

After a record-long federal government shutdown that canceled October's Consumer Price Index (CPI) release, November's report unexpectedly showed a sharp decline in inflation. Headline U.S. inflation eased to +2.7% year over year, while core CPI fell to +2.6%, marking the slowest pace of price growth in nearly five years. Price growth decelerated across most consumer categories, offsetting lingering price pressures from energy, food, and shelter. While the incomplete November data warrants caution until December's figures are available, the report suggested continued progress toward the Fed's 2% annualized inflation target. Despite stabilizing home prices and growing inventory, the U.S. housing market remained stagnant due to affordability constraints, elevated mortgage rates, and slowing job growth.

In December, the U.S. Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) lowered the federal funds rate by 25 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%), to a new target range of 3.50–3.75%, marking its third consecutive 25-bp rate cut. The move reflected signs of slowing U.S. economic growth and emerging labor market risks. However, the Fed signaled a pause, noting it will assess “the extent and timing of additional adjustments to the target range.” Current projections call for only one additional 25-bp rate cut for 2026, which would lower the federal funds rate to approximately 3.40% by the end of 2026. The Fed lifted its 2026 U.S. real gross domestic product growth forecast to 2.3% from 1.8%, while keeping its unemployment projection unchanged at 4.4%. Core Personal Consumption Expenditures Index inflation is now expected to ease to 3.0% in 2025 and then decline further to 2.5% in 2026 and 2.1% in 2027.

Fund performance and attribution

Top Contributors And Detractors To Quarter-End Fund Performance

  • Fox Corp. (FOX)

  • United Therapeutics Corp. (UTHR)

Detractors

  • Nutanix, Inc. (NTNX)
  • Netflix, Inc. (NFLX)
  • Generac Holdings, Inc. (GNRC)
  • Pinterest, Inc. (PINS)
  • Owens Corning (OC)

The holdings identified do not represent all of the securities purchased or sold during the period shown and should not be construed as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. Information on calculation methodology and a list showing the overall contribution of each holding in the account for the period shown are available upon request.

The Large Cap Core Fund returned 0.76% for the quarter while the benchmark S&P 500 Index returned 2.66%. The fund's underperformance was attributed primarily to negative stock selection within the industrials, IT, and energy sectors. Positive stock selection within the real estate, financials, and consumer staples sectors contributed to performance.

A leading contributor for the quarter was Twilio. Twilio is a leading provider in the growing communications platform-as-a-service market offering a robust cloud-based platform and proprietary application programming interfaces that enable developers to integrate voice, video, text, and email capabilities into their own software applications. Key cloud communication clients include Lyft, American Red Cross, Airbnb, Salesforce, and Twitter. Twilio delivered strong third quarter results with revenue growth accelerating 15% year over year on broad-based strength across customer segments, products, and geographies. Active customer accounts expanded to 392,000, driven by notable growth in the messaging, voice, voice AI, and verify segments. Investors were further encouraged as management raised fiscal-year 2025 guidance for revenue, profitability, and free cash flow while also announcing the acquisition of Styxch, an identity and authentication platform for AI agents. Twilio's market leadership, developer-centric model, operational discipline, and scalable product suite leave the company well positioned amid an evolving cloud and AI-driven engagement landscape.

Netflix, Inc., the world's leading streaming platform serving nearly 300 million subscribers across 190 countries, was a detractor for the quarter. The company's content is available on a wide range of devices, including PCs, TVs, gaming consoles, and smartphones. Netflix reported mixed third-quarter results with revenue growth meeting expectations and advertising revenue reaching a record high. However, investors were disappointed as operating margins and earnings due fell short due to a tax dispute in Brazil.

Performance was supported by robust ad sales, steady subscriber growth, strong user engagement, and recent price increases. Despite these positives, the stock came under pressure as management issued cautious fourth quarter 2025 revenue guidance and investors weighed potential costs and regulatory hurdles tied to Netflix's proposed acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery. Looking ahead, Netflix is poised to benefit from the global proliferation of internet-connected devices and preference for on-demand video. Netflix's growth outlook remains strong, supported by a dual revenue model of subscriptions and a rapidly scaling ad-supported tier. Subscriber momentum continues from password-sharing crackdowns and strategic price increases, while engagement is reinforced by hit content and expansion into live events such as the NFL, WWE, and boxing. Strong financials, including rising operating margins, significant free cash flow, and aggressive share buyback, underscore Netflix's ability to return capital while funding growth. Coupled with AI-driven ad innovations and a premium content slate, Netflix remains well positioned for sustained revenue acceleration and margin expansion.

Fund positioning and outlook

As we enter 2026, U.S. equity markets are navigating a fine line between bullish optimism and growing investor caution. U.S. stocks remain near record highs, supported by resilient corporate earnings, sustained momentum in AI innovation, and a more accommodative Fed. Three rate cuts in 2025 and expectations for additional easing in 2026 have bolstered valuations, particularly in growth-oriented sectors. Despite this positive backdrop, the elevated valuations of mega-cap technology stocks leave little margin for error. Investors remain vigilant about persistent inflation, tariff risks, margin pressures, and signs of labor market weakness. AI-driven productivity gains are expected to broaden, but market volatility could rise sharply if AI momentum falters and investors start demanding greater compensation for risk.

Despite a strong rebound since April's "Liberation Day" sell-off, U.S. stocks continued to trail major global indexes, partly due to a weaker dollar. If trade negotiations progress and tariffs stabilize, lower rates and accelerating AI-driven productivity should enable U.S. equities to regain leadership versus international markets. A reduction in policy uncertainty could support a dollar recovery and refocus investor attention on the long-term potential of AI-led growth. While trade policy remains in the hands of President Trump, we suspect markets may act as a guardrail again if tariff tensions reescalate.

In May of 2025, Moody's downgraded the U.S. credit rating, citing rising deficits from higher debt service costs and new spending initiatives. Just weeks later, Congress passed the One Big Beautiful Bill, a sweeping package of trillions in spending and tax cuts that reignited concerns about ballooning fiscal deficits and the long-term sustainability of the U.S. national debt. The U.S. federal deficit, which briefly narrowed to $1.4 trillion in 2022, is now projected to exceed $1.9 trillion in the 2025 fiscal year, which is roughly 6% of GDP. This deterioration comes as unemployment hovers near 4% and the debt-to-GDP ratio approaches 125%, underscoring mounting fiscal pressures despite a relatively strong labor market.

While U.S. inflation is expected to ease in 2026, reaching the Fed's 2% target will likely take longer than anticipated. Trade policy uncertainty has led businesses and consumers to adopt a "wait-and-see" approach. Tariffs are unlikely to cause a U.S. recession if their revenue can be effectively reinvested into the economy, but tariffs may contribute to slower GDP growth and place upward pressure on core CPI inflation. Loose fiscal policy may be partially offset by government job cuts and the Fed's data-dependent approach to rate adjustments. A further moderation in service prices and wage growth may be necessary to move inflation closer to the Fed's target. Persistent inflation combined with solid U.S. GDP growth is expected to limit the Fed to just one 25-bp cut in 2026. In the meantime, governments, corporations, investors, and households will continue adapting to an environment of elevated interest rates.

Consensus estimates currently project 15% earnings growth for the S&P 500 Index in fiscal-year 2026, with the index trading at a forward P/E of approximately 23 times. While this is not necessarily a bubble valuation level, the S&P 500 Index's market capitalization has rarely been this high as a share of global GDP. Despite mega-cap technology stocks leading once again in 2025, equity returns broadened out relative to 2023 and 2024, and investors are increasingly being rewarded for diversifying further down the market-cap spectrum. U.S. small- and mid-cap stocks remain attractively valued relative to large caps, offering compelling upside potential amid broader market uncertainty. We expect this market environment to provide opportunities for disciplined stock selection to add value as it did throughout 2025.

Given the economic, geopolitical, and market backdrop, we anticipate volatile and range-bound market conditions in the near term. We will neutralize our exposures to macroeconomic and geopolitical risks where possible. While we are cautious, we will look to take advantage of market volatility to invest in companies with strong fundamentals at reasonable valuations. As we monitor the macroeconomic environment, we will continue to diligently focus on company fundamentals and disciplined portfolio risk management. From a sector perspective, the portfolio is currently overweight health care, energy, and real estate while it is underweight consumer discretionary, utilities, and financials.

Benchmark descriptions:

The S&P 500 Index consists of 500 stocks chosen for market size, liquidity, and industry group representation. It is a market-value-weighted index with each stock's weight in the index proportionate to its market value. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Russell 2000® Index measures the performance of the 2,000 smallest companies in the Russell 3000® Index, which represents approximately 8% of the total market capitalization of the Russell 3000 Index. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Russell 2500™ Index measures the performance of the small- to mid-cap segment of the U.S. equity universe. It is a subset of the Russell 3000® Index and includes approximately 2,500 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market capitalization and current index membership. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) World ex USA Index (NET) is a free-float-adjusted market-capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed markets, excluding the U.S. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) Emerging Markets Index (NET) is a free-float-adjusted market-capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure equity market performance of emerging markets. You cannot invest directly in an index.

Source: MSCI. MSCI makes no express or implied warranties or representations and shall have no liability whatsoever with respect to any MSCI data contained herein. The MSCI data may not be further redistributed or used as a basis for other indexes or any securities or financial products. This report is not approved, reviewed, or produced by MSCI.

The Consumer Price Index (CPI) is a measure that examines the weighted average of prices of a basket of consumer goods and services, such as transportation, food, and medical care. It is calculated by taking price changes for each item in the predetermined basket of goods and averaging them. Changes in the CPI are used to assess price changes associated with the cost of living. The CPI is one of the most frequently used statistics for identifying periods of inflation or deflation. You cannot invest directly in an index.

U.S. headline inflation is the raw inflation figure reported through the Consumer Price Index (CPI) that is released monthly by the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS). The CPI calculates the cost to purchase a fixed basket of goods to determine how much inflation is occurring in the broad economy. The CPI uses a base year and indexes the current year's prices, according to the base year's values. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Core Consumer Price Index (CPI) is a measure that examines the weighted average of prices of a basket of consumer goods and services, but excludes food and energy. It is calculated by taking price changes for each item in the predetermined basket of goods and averaging them. Changes in the Core CPI are used to assess price changes associated with the cost of living. The CPI is one of the most frequently used statistics for identifying periods of inflation or deflation. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) Index is the primary measure of consumer spending on goods and services in the U.S. economy. It accounts for about two-thirds of domestic final spending and is part of the personal income report issued by the Bureau of Economic Analysis of the Department of Commerce. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

The Morningstar Category average is the average return for the peer group based on the returns of each individual fund within the group. The total return of the Morningstar Category average does not include the effect of sales charges. You cannot invest directly in a Morningstar Category average.

Diversification does not ensure or guarantee better performance and cannot eliminate the risk of investment losses.

Attribution analysis:

Performance attribution and sector returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. As such, performance attribution calculations may differ from the fund's actual investment results. Common sources of attribution dispersion from official returns include: the use of end of day holdings and prices, the exclusion of fees, and the use of myriad pricing sources to accommodate different benchmark providers.

Price/earnings (P/E) is the price of a share of a stock divided by earnings per share, usually calculated using the latest year's earnings.

Definition of terms:

Alpha measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund's historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund's performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund's performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund's standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund's historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment's performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager's performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund's performance and a benchmark's performance.

Upside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Risks: Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Stock values fluctuate in response to the activities of individual companies and general market and economic conditions. Consult the fund's prospectus for additional information on these and other risks.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.

Across U.S.-domiciled large blend funds, the Large Cap Core Fund received 4 stars among 1,210 funds, 5 stars among 1,122 funds, and 3 stars among 880 funds for the 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods, respectively. data.title: NVDU As AI Stocks Dip
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) dominates AI hardware with GPUs essential for AI model training. For non-technical investors like me, this means NVDA produces very sophisticated computer chips that are necessary to support artificial intelligence, given its massive hardware power and a near-monopoly in the software ecosystem.

According to NVDA’s CEO, Jensen Huang, “The ChatGPT moment for physical AI is here when machines begin to understand, reason, and act in the real world.” He also warned that “You won’t lose your job to AI; you’ll lose your job to someone who uses AI.”

After reaching a record high in October 2025, NVDA shares have pulled back and consolidated. Over the past weeks, weakness in the technology sector has raised concerns that NVDA shares could be overdue for a correction. However, the company’s leadership role and virtual monopoly increase the odds that higher highs will continue. The Direxion Daily NVDA Bull 2X Shares ETF (NVDU) could be a useful product ahead of the company’s announcement of earnings later this month.

Nvidia Remains the Most Valuable Company in the World

NVDA continues to lead the world in market capitalization.

The chart shows that the top 10 companies by market capitalization all had market caps over $1.5 trillion. NVDA leads the pack with a value three times that of $4.45 trillion, $691 billion more than second-place Apple (AAPL).

AI Supports a Higher NVDA Share Price

NDVA’s company profile states:

In the technology sector, artificial intelligence is the holy grail. NVDA produces the chips supporting AI. Some of the highlights of NVDA’s AI-driven focus are:

The Compute & Networking segment includes its Data Centre accelerated computing platforms, artificial intelligence solutions, and software.

Quadro/Nvidia RTX GPUs include Omniverse software for building and operating AI and digital twin applications. It also provides customized agentic solutions, designed in collaboration with NVDA, to accelerate enterprise AI adoption.

The company also has a strategic partnership with Siemens Aktiengesellschaft (SIEGY) to develop industrial AI solutions for AI-driven innovation in every industry and industrial workflow.

AI-related Stocks Have Corrected

Many AI-related stocks have experienced downside corrections over the past weeks. NVDA’s correction began in late October 2025 at its all-time high of $212.19 per share.

The chart shows that NVDA shares fell 20% from the October 29, 2025, high of $212.19 to the November 25 low of $169.55 per share. Meanwhile, in a bullish sign for the stock, NVDA has made higher lows on December 17, 2025, and most recently on February 5, 2026. At the $182.81 level on February 13, the stock was trading below the midpoint of the October through November 2025 trading range.

NVDA Will Report Earnings in Late February- a $5 Trillion Market Cap Could Be on the Horizon

NVDA will report earnings on February 25, 2026:

The chart shows that NVDA beat EPS and revenue forecasts in November, and analysts have raised their expectations ahead of the February 25 report.

Meanwhile, NVDA has a history of beating analyst forecasts on earnings reports:

As the chart shows, the company will need to continue producing substantial revenue and earnings growth to keep pace with future consensus forecasts.

NVDA’s Seeking Alpha Factor Grades continue to earn a failing grade in valuation due to its sky-high price-to-earnings ratio of nearly 39 times earnings. However, profitability consistently earns the highest possible A+, while growth prospects receive an A. The stock’s lethargic price action over the past months earns a B grade in momentum, while NVDA earns a B+ grade in the revisions category.

NVDA’s next line in the sand comes at the end of this month when it releases its earnings report.

NVDU Is a Short-term Trading Vehicle That Turbocharges NVDA Share Performance

NVDA is a market leader, and the path of least resistance of its stock leads other technology and AI companies. After the recent downside correction, another forecast-beating earnings report could flip the script for another leg up in AI and technology stocks.

The Direxion Daily NVDA Bull 2X Shares ETF is a leveraged product that turbocharges the price action in NVDA shares. At $110.15 per share, NVDU had over $583 million in assets under management. NVDU trades an average of over 469,000 shares per day and charges a 0.97% management fee. According to Seeking Alpha’s metrics, NVDU’s dividend yield is 6.06%.

NVDA shares rose 145% from the April 2025 low of $86.62 to the October 2025 high of $212.19 per share before correcting 20% to the November 2025 $169.55 per share low.

Over the same period, the leveraged NVDU ETF rose 408% from the April 2025 low of $32.63 to the October 2025 high of $165.78 per share, then corrected 41.3% to the February 2026 low of $97.39 per share.

NVDU’s leverage, creating time decay, earns a failing grade in the risk category. The bearish trend and high management fee receive D- grades, while the daily volume and assets under management earn an A- in the liquidity category. If Seeking Alpha’s dividend metric is correct, the D in the dividend category is the only questionable grade.

NVDU is a tool suitable only for short-term risk positions on the long side of NVDA shares. When approaching leveraged NVDA exposure with NVDU, time and price stops are appropriate, as the gearing can lead to oversized losses. Moreover, stable NVDA share prices will likely cause NVDU to decline, as time decay erodes its value. The swaps and options that create leverage dramatically increase risks. The SEC website highlights the significant risks associated with leveraged ETFs, such as NVDU.

Meanwhile, with earnings on the horizon, NVDA’s history of beating the forecasts, and the recent correction in AI and technology-related stocks, NVDU could be a valuable tool to magnify NVDA’s stock performance over the coming weeks if another earnings beat pushes the stock higher.

data.title: PGIM Strategic Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

  • With 2026 underway, a few factors are readily apparent across the global fixed income markets: the slow-going bull market remains in the sweet spot; attractive yield levels should continue to accrue into solid returns over the intermediate to longer term; and the unusual geopolitical backdrop and asynchronous central bank cycles should continue to create opportunities to add value through active management.
  • Although events like Q1's tariff unveiling and accompanying market swoon created bumps along the way, the ongoing economic expansion with moderate growth and inflation has kept yields generally high and range bound—an environment where the highest-yielding sectors continued to post the highest returns.
  • Although excess returns from spread products were once again positive in 2025—as they have been throughout the three years of the bull market—they were more muted. Spreads are narrower, and the bulk of the capital gains potential from narrowing spreads is well behind us at this point of the cycle.
  • Thanks to the positive yield curve and a slight drop in yields, Treasuries finally outperformed cash and joined the bull market last year. Two prevailing themes from late 2025—renewed divergence across global monetary policy rates and the repricing of term premia—will likely remain at play within developed market rate complexes with 2026 underway. So, while we expect a smaller return contribution from spread product going forward, long-term fixed income should pick up a tailwind from the yield advantage and roll down benefits provided by the newly positive yield curves.
  • In the U.S., the Fed's 25 bp rate cut in December carried a dovish tone, leading to a bull steepening along the curve with only slight movement in the 10-year yield. The narrow move on the 10-year underscored its prevailing low-volatility, range-bound conditions throughout 2025. The low-volatility conditions in the U.S. were further amplified by the selloff across other developed market yield curves late last year. Indeed, at the front of the curve, the 2-year U.S. yield outperformed its global counterparts by a wide margin as the end of several policy easing cycles came into sight.
  • Over the coming 12 months, our “Muddle Through” base case for the U.S. sees further weakening in the labor market against a backdrop of stubbornly high inflation between 2.5- 3.0%. The Fed, torn between the conflicting signals of its dual mandate, will likely chart a patient path to the estimated neutral range of monetary policy (i.e., 3.0-3.25%) in 2026. While not our base case, once a new Fed chair is confirmed, the potential exists for the policy rate to decline towards a sub-neutral range of 2.0-2.5% in the second half of 2026.
  • In Europe, our base case also anticipates near-trend growth (~1.2- 1.4%) as rising real incomes and fiscal support underpin the consumer, while exports and investment remain challenged. A mild, but extended, period where inflation undershoots the ECB's 2% target may incentivize the ECB to implement another 25 bps rate cut to bring the deposit rate to the lower side of neutral at 1.75%.
  • While we can hardly be certain as to what this year will bring, our a priori is more of the same: high and range bound yields look set to persist, allowing this slow-go bull market to continue, where returns accrue not by a quick drop in yields and rise in prices, but rather thanks to the ongoing earning of yield itself.
  • Additionally, albeit by thinner margins and with more idiosyncratic risk, spread product looks set to deliver positive, but perhaps narrower, excess returns. Additionally, newly positive yield curves should give long-term fixed income a performance advantage relative to cash in the quarters ahead.
  • We remain guardedly optimistic on entry into what stands to be year four of the “yield is destiny” bond bull market, where high and range bound yields result in solid returns, and confusion creates ample opportunities for adding value through active management. Given a wide spectrum of risks and outcomes, vigilance remains in order.

See back page for methodology which takes into account Lipper rankings. PGIM Strategic Bond Fund (Class Z) Lipper total return ranking for the 1-, 3-, and 5-year periods as of 12/31/2024 for the Multi-Sector Income Fund category were: 115/377, 240/351, and 212/305, respectively. Lipper Funds category rankings are based on total return, do not take sales charges into account, and are calculated against all funds in each fund's respective Lipper category. Lipper total return ranking for the 1-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods as of 12/31/2025: 113/387, 147/370, 234/329, and 45/224, respectively. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

4Q 2025 Performance

  • The Fund outperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Intermediate Aggregate Bond Index, on a gross basis.
  • Overall security selection was the largest contributor to performance during the period, with selection in U.S. investment grade corporates, U.S. high yield corporates, non-agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS), and European bank loans contributing the most. This was partially offset by selection in GBP high yield corporates, AA collateralized loan obligations (s), emerging-market high yield, and U.S. bank loans, which detracted the most.
  • Overall sector allocation also contributed to performance, with overweights to the emerging-market high yield, AA, and non-agency CMBS AA-and-below sectors, along with an underweight to the U.S. investment grade corporate sector contributing the most. This was partially offset by overweights to the U.S. high yield corporate and GBP high yield corporate sectors, along with an underweight to the MBS sector, which detracted.
  • Within credit, positioning in media & entertainment, gaming/lodging/leisure, and healthcare & pharmaceuticals, contributed the most to results. Positioning in telecom and cable & satellite detracted.
  • From a market perspective, having more risk in the Fund, on average, than the relevant benchmark over the period contributed to performance. In aggregate, the Fund's duration and yield curve positioning contributed to performance as front-end yields on the U.S. Treasury yields declined.

Top-Down Macro Positioning

Duration (Current: 4.4 yrs, Previous Quarter: 4.5 yrs)

  • We are inclined to express our duration views through the options markets. Q4's low-volatility trajectory is unlikely to continue in 2026. Our economic scenarios have the widest and thickest tails in recent memory, yet the markets have coalesced around a very narrow distribution of outcomes. This seems inconsistent and, as a result, we expect volatility to move higher in 2026.
  • Notably, in the U.S., Chair Powell's term will conclude in May 2026. However, the tension regarding the Fed likely raises the hurdle for the next Fed Chair to establish monetary-policy credibility and to sustain the perception of independence.
  • At this point, the market is pricing in a Fed funds rate of 3.0% by the end of 2026. A push into accommodative territory is likely to require a combination of a more dovish Fed voting slate and/or a significant slowing in growth and/or inflation. While not our base case, once a new Fed chair is confirmed, the potential exists for the policy rate to decline towards a sub-neutral range of 2.0-2.5% in the second half of 2026.

Curve Positioning

  • The 2022 selloff left yields at respectable levels—which remains largely intact—with an unusual net result: a potentially long-lived bull market primarily driven by the accretion of income, rather than a short bull market fueled by a quick drop in yields. Long-term fixed income should pick up a tailwind from the yield advantage and roll down benefits provided by newly positive yield curves. In the U.S., the positive yield curve and range-bound market highlight two themes: 1) bonds should outperform cash on yield; and 2) swings in sentiment should lift duration management as a tactical alpha driver.
  • Steeper yield curves should remain prominent globally. The long end of developed-market sovereign curves have become more sensitive to fiscal news. Geopolitical rivalry is expensive. Record defense budgets, expanding industrial policy, and rising populism will compel major economies to run historically high deficits. Accompanying demands for low monetary policy rates erodes central banks' inflation-fighting credibility. Repriced term premia will increasingly determine the level of long-term rates.
  • In the EA, the top down growth story will be driven primarily by strong government support, both in spending and investment, masking weak private sector dynamism. Our base case is that quarterly GDP growth will gently accelerate, reflecting inertia in the periphery and stronger activity in France and Germany on the back of higher fiscal spending.

Bottom-Up Sector Positioning

  • In Q4, credit products generally posted positive total returns, with spreads for investment grade corporates remaining tight and high yield spreads unchanged, amid strong technicals and stable fundamentals. Securitized credit spreads were mixed, with high-quality commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) tightening and collateralized loan obligations (s) widening over the quarter. In addition, emerging-market hard currency, local rates, and corporates posted strong total returns as spreads tightened.
  • The current environment continues to create distortions in the interest rate, sector, issuer, and currency realms. This results in risks, but also opportunities to add value through active management across the fixed income asset spectrum.
  • The trend of compressing credit spreads should generally continue, albeit more modestly given the tight spread levels. These conditions describe “a carry market” if there ever was one. In terms of positioning, we see sovereign bonds as more attractive than credit. Consequently, we continue to advocate for lighter risk and are focused on carry-type instruments.

Corporate Credit

Investment-Grade Corporates (Current: 10.7%, Previous Quarter 10.1%)

  • A resilient economy in 2025 and attractive yields made for a roughly unchanged Index and carry-like returns in the U.S. IG market. Other contributing factors included robust inflows, manageable gross and net issuance, and generally stable fundamentals. Heading into 2026, leverage has been stable, and profit margins are near historical highs.
  • We expect the economy and yields to remain favorable, with strong earnings growth from the S&P 500. That stated, the very strong technical picture from 2025 is expected to weaken with increased gross and net supply, arising from growing capex, especially from the Tech sector. In addition, the backdrop is favorable for M&A and this corporate behavior could weaken credit fundamentals.
  • Broader-based earnings growth is expected. Unlike in prior years, growth for 2026 will not be concentrated in the “Mag 7.” In fact, EPS is expected to grow ~12% YoY in 2026, with slightly more than half of that growth coming from the S&P 493.
  • Spreads on the U.S. IG Index remain tight, credit curves are flat, and BBBs are tight to As. Increased supply—attributed to a pickup in M&A and capex activity—could cause credit curves to steepen in 2026. While it is uncertain how aggressive management teams will be with balance sheets, more credit dispersion is expected. Overall, we believe spreads on the Index will likely trade within a 90-95 bps range.
  • Between $1.8- $2.2 trillion in gross issuance and $850-$950 billion in net issuance is anticipated in 2026. Most of the supply will come from higher-quality, non-financial issuers in technology (e.g., hyperscalers), utilities (related to the AI buildout), pipelines, and pharmaceuticals (tariff-related).
  • In terms of position, we favor banks, technology, and select utilities. We are underweight life insurance, chemicals, and pharmaceuticals. Potential Risks include slowing economic growth, fiscal deficits, inflation, Fed policy, geopolitical uncertainty, rate volatility, and corporate event risk.

High Yield Corporates (Current: 17.1%, Previous Quarter 19.3%)

  • We expect U.S. HY-bond spreads to remain range-bound, near historic tights, over the coming months due to strong technicals and robust credit fundamentals. HY spreads entered a late-cycle phase—i.e., an environment of tight spreads and low volatility—that could last throughout much of 2026.
  • While relatively tame “animal spirits” have curtailed primary supply. We expect increased M&A, LBO, and AI-related capex transactions in 2026, which limits any meaningful spread tightening from current levels. That stated, the sector should still generate positive total returns in the coming quarters, particularly as a potential rise in defaults amid re-leveraging and more aggressive behavior could be years away.
  • Solid market technicals should persist beyond Q1, supported by the healthy pace of refinancing, low speculative borrowing, and modest inflows. Further, the continued strength in credit fundamentals provides a buffer against macro uncertainty. However, geopolitical risks remain, while potential overheating from monetary and fiscal stimulus could push spreads wider, and the use of PE dry powder could reignite speculative issuance.
  • In terms of sectors, we are maintaining our overweight to short-duration bonds and continue to reduce our underweight to high-quality issues. Looking at sectors, we are overweight home construction, telecom, and midstream energy, and underweight technology, media & entertainment, and retailers & restaurants. However, recent adjustments include an increase to our overweight in paper & packaging, a further reduction in our overweights to building materials, home construction, and telecom, and an increase in our underweight to technology

Bank Loans (Current: 1.1%, Previous Quarter 1.1%)

  • In 2026, we expect returns to be driven primarily by carry, but tempered by downward pressure from lower base rates, continued repricing transactions, elevated credit concerns, and potential technical pressures if net issuance accelerates. While we do not expect broad-based deterioration in fundamentals, defaults and LMEs should remain elevated with recoveries below historical averages.
  • We continue to favor public BB and high B loans over sponsor-owned, low B and CCC loans as we expect those lower-quality facilities to be challenged by the fundamental backdrop. We believe that deep, fundamental credit research/modeling is increasingly important to credit selection. To that end, avoidance of defaults will likely be the biggest driver of alpha over the next 12-24 months.

SECURITIZED (ABS)

Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities (CMBS) (Current: 7.2%, Previous Quarter 7.0%)

  • Valuations for most property types have stabilized, and we expect price appreciation to be flat for the year—likely no V-shaped recovery given long-term rate expectations. Rent growth continues normalizing across property types and may increase as supply pressures abate but will likely stay within range of historical averages.
  • Elevated single-asset, single-borrower (SASB) supply has contributed to attractive spreads, and we see value in high-quality deals with structural protections. In conduit markets, we favor shorter spread duration given the current term-curve profile

Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs) (Current: 9.9%, Previous Quarter 10.9%)

  • In CLOs, senior tranches continue to offer attractive relative value compared to many fixed income asset classes, while selectively adding higher-quality mezzanine tranches. With CLO spreads still inside of their long-term averages, but wide of 2025 tights, we expect mixed valuations in the near term. This should create entry points at wider levels.
  • CLO fundamentals are improving—supported by growth in revenue and earnings as well as generally positive outlooks—but tail risks remain, i.e., more than 10% of underlying collateral features stressed interest-coverage ratios (below 1.5x).

Other ABS (Current: 1.3%, Previous Quarter 1.4%)

  • In asset-backed securities (ABS), the consumer remains stretched—especially the non-prime cohort—as they take on more debt and elevated default rates continue in seasoned ABS trusts. Considering the spread compression globally and pronounced credit tiering, we’re positioned toward top-tier originators.
  • We favor bank originated collateral, defined vs. general purpose loans, short-dated consumer loans, and commercial obligor exposures. We’re constructive on select, higher-quality issuers across the capital stack within auto, consumer loans, and commercial sectors, offering favorable relative value within a global opportunity set.

Non-U.S. Bonds

Foreign Government Related (Current: 0.1%, Previous Quarter 0.1%)

  • As 2026 opens, we have a guardedly optimistic view on developed market rates, with a caveat. In Europe, long-term bunds have sold off, and we're monitoring whether yields may soon crest or if they are signaling a demand issue at the back of the curve. The ECB held its deposit rate at 2.0% to end 2025, and it expects an inflation undershoot in 2026 and 2027, which supports the potential for an additional 25 bp cut to 1.75% this year in what could represent the end of the easing cycle.
  • Policy easing should continue in the UK after the Bank of England's late-year rate cut brought its policy rate to 3.75%. Looking ahead, we see three additional 25 bp cuts in the first half of 2026 amidst the sizable slack in the UK economy, a budget that may limit inflation pressures, and potential improvement in the country's fiscal conditions in the first half of the year.
  • The steepening trend was clearly visible in the JGB market late last year as the Bank of Japan hiked its policy rate by 25 bps to 0.75%, marking the highest level in 30 years. The BoJ's statement that "real interest rates are at significantly low levels," indicated that it may be setting its sights on the lower end of its estimated neutral range of 1.0-2.5%, meaning that additional hikes may materialize in 2026. We anticipate further steepening along the 2- to 10-year portion of the JGB curve.

Emerging Markets Debt (EMD) (Current: 8.6%, Previous Quarter 10.6%)

  • EM Sovereigns/Spreads—The EM/DM growth differential should continue to play in EM's favor, even as China's growth slows. A growth differential of 2.5% or higher often leads to flows into EMD. Within the global backdrop, the extent to which slowing growth is still "good enough" and whether relative EM growth continues to attract flows, all matter. However, country-specific drivers should dominate in 2026. In a context where the Fed cuts rates and the U.S. fiscal outlook does not result in a massive bear steepening of the U.S. Treasury curve, EM hard currency returns could reach the high-single digit range with the appropriate identification of bottom-up alpha opportunities.
  • We continue to see attractive opportunities in BBs as they are well insulated and offer a yield cushion. "Stressed" BB names either have support from the U.S., other bilaterals, and/or multilaterals, as well as constructive fundamental dynamics.
  • Other geopolitical hotspots, including Ukraine and Venezuela, remain complicated. While vast uncertainty surrounds Venezuelan-related bonds, Maduro's ousting may be considered a market-positive event. For now, the current Venezuelan leadership may cooperate with the U.S. on potential investment in the oil sector and with immigration issues. At a minimum, this puts possible, yet complex, debt restructuring on the horizon
  • EMFX—We remain relative-value focused with the expectation that high-carry currencies will continue to outperform amid a broad-based weakening in the U.S. dollar due to a weakening U.S. labor market, additional Fed rate cuts, ample U.S. liquidity; a "Muddle Through" U.S. economy that should benefit high-carry currencies and EU fiscal measures that could offset growth downside and lead to higher growth vis-a-vis the U.S. Risks to this outlook include fewer Fed rate cuts than anticipated, a U.S. recession or global growth slowdown, increased geopolitical risks, and fiscal and election concerns in select countries, such as Brazil and Colombia.
  • EM Local Markets—After stellar performance in 2025, we have turned cautious on EM local rates. The most notable feature among many EM countries last year was monetary easing, bringing terminal rates closer to the lower end of the neutral rate zone. Brazil was the only EM country that hiked in 2025. Our base case in 2026 is that the majority of countries that cut rates last year are likely to stay on hold for the foreseeable future as reflected by the positive 2s5s spread on the swap curve.
  • EM Corporates—Spreads remained well anchored in Q4, driving positive returns. We believe this will likely remain the case in Q1 given the strong fundamentals and still elevated spread pick-up to developed market HY. While EM corporates significantly lagged sovereigns over the past quarter and year, this came after a period of strong outperformance. We expect overall defaults to remain contained in the 3% area for EM HY going forward (in line with developed markets) given very manageable refinancing needs and resiliency in the underlying EM economies.

U.S. Government Related

U.S. Treasuries (Current: 17.1%, Previous Quarter 9.5%)

  • The Fed's 25 bp rate cut in December carried a dovish tone, leading to a bull steepening along the curve with only slight movement in the 10-year yield. The narrow move on the 10-year underscored its prevailing low-volatility, range-bound conditions throughout 2025.
  • Thanks to the positive yield curve and a slight drop in yields, Treasuries outperformed cash and joined the bull market last year.
  • The Fed concluded its balance sheet normalization in December. In terms of supply, the U.S. Treasury has resisted calls to issue more long-term debt, instead opting to fund larger deficits through short-dated T-Bills, while also carrying out limited Treasury buybacks along the yield curve.

U.S. Mortgage-Backed Securities (Current: 20.6%, Previous Quarter 26.1%)

  • Agency MBS posted positive excess returns vs. U.S. Treasuries in Q4, benefitting from lower volatility, improved demand, and light origination volumes despite the general decline in mortgage rates. Refinancing activity rose during the period and prepayment speeds increased—the brunt of which was borne by higher coupons within GNMA and conventional MBS.
  • The sector starts Q1 with tighter valuations relative to early Q4 due to easing Fed policy, lower volatility, constrained origination, and persistent demand for carry. While December's FOMC decision supported risk assets, further spread compressions will be difficult. We see a potential opportunity to outperform the benchmark index by avoiding the realized prepayment speeds in high coupon premiums.

Hacker Interstellar Is Producing Uneven Results

Hacker Interstellar Inc. (SOUD) has filed to raise $15 million in a U.S. IPO of its ordinary shares, according to an F-1 registration statement.

The company is developing social network voice and AI-related technologies for internet users in emerging markets.

However, in the most recent half-year period, user numbers are falling, as are ARPU and financial results.

I’ll provide an update when we gain more information about the IPO’s pricing and valuation.

What Does Hacker Interstellar Do?

The company is developing and commercializing a suite of voice-based AI technologies based on large language models (LLMs) and adapting them for vernacular languages and dialects, principally in Asian, Southeast Asian, and Middle-Eastern countries.

SOUD’s technologies include:

  • Voice social app - YoYo.

  • AI/LLM voice model technologies.

  • AI multilingual recommendation engine.

What Is Hacker Interstellar’s Market?

The AI voice generator market was an estimated $4.2 billion in 2025 and is expected to exceed $20.7 billion by 2031.

If achieved, this would represent a CAGR of 30.7% from 2025 to 2031, a very high rate of growth.

Expected drivers for this growth include organizations adopting voice technologies for low-latency speech, multilingual voice models, and real-time personalization.

Media platforms are projected to adopt these technologies to enhance engagement.

In 2025, North America was believed to hold the highest market share at around 41% of the total.

Hacker Interstellar is active on the consumer media subsegment and within emerging markets by region.

Competitors to the company include existing legacy social networks and local online social networking and entertainment industry market participants.

The firm’s strengths include its modified AI voice technology, localization focus (23 languages to date), strong user engagement of an average of 75 minutes per session, and internal AI automated operations system

Its weaknesses are its geographic concentration in politically and economically unstable regions, mobile platform dependency, and other concentration risks (supplier, technology)

Opportunities for the company include its plans to enter new regional markets, enterprise applications, and AI agent integrations

But SOUD faces ongoing uncertainties with the regulatory environment in China and intense competition from legacy players with large existing user bases.

Hacker Interstellar’s Recent Financial Results

The company's revenue growth has been uneven, falling YoY in the most recent half-year period, while gross profit and gross margin have also dropped recently.

Operating and net results have also trended lower, while cash flow from operations has bounced back in the most recent reporting period:

The chart below shows that gross margin has trended lower, but so have SG&A and R&D costs:

Hacker Interstellar’s IPO Plan

SOUD has filed to sell $15 million of its ordinary shares, although the final figure may change.

Since Nasdaq changed its minimum value of unrestricted publicly held shares (MVUPHS) from $5 million to $15 million on January 16, 2026, many small companies have been knocked out of the IPO process, and the remaining candidates have had to increase their IPOs to at least $15 million.

The firm, directors, and shareholders (5% or greater) will be subject to the standard 180-day shareholder sale lockup period after the effective date of the prospectus.

The company plans to use the IPO’s net proceeds as follows:

…approximately 50% of the net proceeds, for research and development of our AI agent products, including further development of our core AI algorithms, expansion of our R&D team, and product testing and iteration;

…approximately 30% of the net proceeds, for promotional and marketing activities to increase brand awareness and user acquisition;

…approximately 20% of the net proceeds, for research and development of other digital technologies and products.

(Source - SEC)

Listed underwriters of the IPO are Kingswood Capital Partners, LLC, and Longbridge.

Capital And Liquidity Dynamics

As of June 30, 2025, the company had $60 million in cash, equivalents, and short-term investments and $14 million in total liabilities.

For the trailing twelve months ended June 30, 2025, free cash flow was $15.7 million with no capital expenditures.

Its Selling and R&D spending have been modest for an AI social platform.

However, management will likely use some of the IPO proceeds for greater investments in R&D and potential capex.

The company's balance sheet is strong, with plenty of liquidity available for future growth initiatives, especially when the IPO’s net proceeds of around $11 million are added to its resources.

Hacker Interstellar Is Generating Uneven Results

The company is seeking US public capital market investment to fund its R&D and expansion initiatives.

Its growth plans include expanding regionally into markets such as Latin America and also into B2B applications for its AI-enabled voice technologies.

However, topline revenue has contracted in the most recent half-year period, as have gross profit and net results, while cash flow has been uneven as well.

Its broad market of AI-enabled voice applications is expected to grow at a very high rate of growth in the coming years, so the company enjoys positive industry growth dynamics in its favor.

Hacker Interstellar is a ‘foreign private issuer’ and an ‘emerging growth company,’ which allows management to produce substantially less information for investors.

Many of these types of company stocks have performed poorly in open market trading immediately after their IPO.

Additional risks include its exposure to Chinese government regulatory changes, which can be unpredictable and broadly market-changing.

It also has significant supplier concentration risks and payment platform risks.

Not only has SOUD generated uneven results, but its ARPU (Average Revenue Per User) has also dropped substantially in the most recent reporting period:

This short-term signal is concerning and indicates something may be wrong with the direction the company is pursuing.

So, while the company may be profitable, its falling revenue, reduced ARPU, and substantially lower user numbers may be cause for caution.

I’ll provide a final opinion when we get more IPO details from management.
